What is a monthly meeting? Okay, you're like, oh my gosh, Leila, we have so many meetings now. I promise you they're all unique even though they feel the same. And that is because of the duration of time that they review. So what is a monthly meeting? It's a meeting you have at the beginning of each month to not only remind the team of the vision, culture, and values. Also to review company progress over the last 30 days and to determine the next 30 days priorities. Okay. So a lot of people ask how do you keep your culture strong? I think a big way to that we have done this and I really started doing the beginning and I sucked at first, but it's okay because now I'm great at it. Is that I utilize our monthly meetings. I made them 90 minutes and the first 30 to 40. All I did was talk about the vision and the culture. Okay. And so I can't reiterate to you enough. There's no way I can teach you exactly what to say or do. But it is painting the vision to your team in the beginning of where the company is going, what your goals are, bringing that to life and then saying, and this is what our team needs to look like in order to reach those goals, right? Which is a lot of core values. That being said, if you do that, everyone on your team will always look forward to these rather than you hear out like typical companies that people like, uh, have our monthly meeting or early meeting. I've never heard anyone say that. Everyone's like, I'm so pumped. We have a Monday meeting. Like, let me tell you guys right now, everyone started their new hires. We have nine new people starting on Monday. Why are they all starting on Monday? Typically we like to start midweek because every single person was like, they need to be on that monthly meeting. They need to see that. That's everyone's favorite day. Okay. So make sure it's not just dry and boring. Paint the vision. Talk about the culture and values. Okay. Sell your team on the vision. Sell your team on the culture and values. Okay. Outside of that, reviewing the progress and determining 30 day priorities. Right. So here's an example that we provided to you guys. One, what's the purpose? And this would be so that you all know, I would take this copy and paste it into the meeting invite so that people know what to expect. Okay. So one, review the agenda and desired outcome. So I'd be like, Hey guys, here's what we're going to go over today. We're going to talk about the client-centric mission or core values. I'm going to talk a little bit about the culture. Alice going to paint the vision. We're going to review last month. What our numbers were, the projects we completed. And then we're going to talk about what we're going to do this month. Okay. Cool. Sounds awesome. My desired outcome is that you all know clearly what our team has to do this month. You all understand whose responsibility is to do what you have transparency into what everyone's doing. So you can raise your hand and say, Hey, I want to help. I want to help as well as you guys don't want to call your eyes out. Okay. So that's just like how I might say to my team. Two is share your backstory. Okay. And so this is something that as you're creating your culture cannot be repeated enough. And I can't tell you how many times I've shared our story. I share it in a different way. So I might tell them this version or this perspective that I might come from this, this version of this perspective. The point is that you want to tell a story. You want to engage people in the audience. You want to engage your team. And so if you want to pull them in so they're going to listen to the meeting, you want to share something that's actually enticing. Okay. Which is stories. People like stories. Okay. So for example, always review your client centric mission. I encourage you to have other people in the team read this popcorn at whatever review your core tenants and how they tie into the gym. So another example, this could be you have five core tenants. Say you go through them all and you have to pick somebody who represented one well that month. So say you have your operations manager do it and she had to pick, you know, five people or five incidences that represented the core tenants. Right. And you give kudos to that. Next review your scoreboard from last month versus the intended goals to be like goal number. So goal number. Okay. So what did you want to hit and what did you actually hit? Right. Next is establishing the 30 day goals. So based on what we had last month, what are the goals that we can set for this month? You know, what do we know now that we didn't know then so we can set better goals. Okay. And then you allow the team members to ask questions. So you might intrigue them and encourage them to create these goals by saying, hey guys, what do we have coming up in the next 30 to 90 days? What do you guys think we need to be doing right now? Or, hey guys, what opportunities do you see that we could create to get us closer to our yearly goal like based on our mission of helping 1000 people this year? What do you think we could do in the next 30 days to do that? As well as, hey guys, what do you think is feasible to get done in the next 30 days to move us closer to that goal? You know, do you guys have ideas of things that we could do? You want to, especially in the beginning, if you've never done this, you might feel awkward because there will be silence. That's okay. It's all right. I promise it won't be like that forever. But if you don't continue to ask these questions, regardless of the silence, then you'll never get people to speak up. Okay. Because one day someone will and it'll break the ice and then everyone will start doing it. Lastly, you want to solidify the 30 day goals so you have them all written down, ready that you can email out after or distribute via whatever medium. And basically when you send those out, you want to make sure that you have ownership and deadlines assigned. There's no accountability without ownership and deadlines. Just like y'all run challenges internally with your members. It's like lose this much weight by this date. And here's how you're going to do it. These are the classes you're going to. It needs to be, here's the goal. Here's who's doing it. And here's how they're going to do it by what date. Okay. Same concept. It's human behavior. You need those things to make something happen. And then lastly, you'll recap and close. You'll just recap how last month was, what you're going to do going forward this month, who owns what, and then you let them know next steps like, hey guys, this one let you know. I'm going to send out an email with all this information by end of day today. Look out for it. Print it out. Put it on your desk. Can't wait to see you guys tomorrow. Let's get cranking. And that's it.
